title: Race Morning
description: A Pre-Performance Routine eliminates decision-making when your brain is least equipped.

blocks.content: 4:30 AM. The alarm goes off and for one blissful half-second you don't know why you're awake — then it all comes flooding back. Race day. What most athletes do with the next two hours: panic in slow motion. Check the phone. Check the weather. Eat something while tasting nothing. Drive to the venue. Stand in porta-potty lines. Pin on a number with slightly shaky hands.

At no point in this sequence has anyone made a single deliberate psychological decision. The chimp is running the show from the first alarm buzz. One athlete described the difference between having a protocol and not: 'I went from being inside the car wash to driving through it.' Not the absence of chaos. The presence of a plan to move through it.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a scripted 15-minute pre-race protocol using tools from Modules 1-3.

blocks.question: The most effective strategy for night-before sleep is:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Kolling's review identified pre-sleep relaxation routines as the most effective strategy, and sleep banking across the week matters more than any single night.

options.text: Exhausting yourself with a hard workout the day before
A pre-sleep relaxation routine combined with sleep banking in the week before
Going to bed as early as possible

blocks.content: A Pre-Performance Routine eliminates decision-making when your brain is least equipped. Every decision on race morning costs prefrontal resources — the same resources you'll need in hour four when the race is being decided.
Cross-reference: This protocol assembles tools from across the course — 6-2-7 (1.4), Highlight Reel (3.2), Schema Statement (2.5), Arousal Calibration from Know Your Zone (1.3).

items.label: Minutes 1-5: Breathing Reset (6-2-7)
items.detail: Find a spot — corral, start line, park bench. Do the 6-2-7. Full protocol. Five minutes of slow diaphragmatic breathing. This is the physiological foundation: parasympathetic brake engaged, cortisol dropping, neurological static clearing. Don't shortcut it.

items.label: Minutes 6-8: Highlight Reel Visualization
items.detail: Run the Highlight Reel. The empowering memory, the crucial moment in today's race. First-person, full sensory detail. See yourself EXECUTING, not just finishing. Process imagery, not outcome imagery. This builds on the calm you just created.

items.label: Minutes 9-10: Schema Statement + Performance Statements
items.detail: Say your Athletic Schema Statement from Module 2. The strength and the aspiration. Three times. Slowly. Then load your three Performance Statements — technical, pain-response, and process — into working memory. Identity priming: you're telling your brain who you are before the race tells you.

items.label: Minutes 11-15: Activation Calibration
items.detail: Consult the arousal dial from Know Your Zone. Running hot? Stay with breathing, soften gaze, deploy third-person self-talk. Running cold? Music, explosive movements, power phrases. Already in your zone? Maintain. Butterflies flying in formation is the target.

blocks.content: Commitment: I will practice this protocol before a training session this week as rehearsal.
"You don't rise to the occasion. You fall to the level of your preparation." The routine is the preparation. Practice it before you need it.
Next — the first hour, where most races are lost.
